div.head: The Chief of the American Representation on the Preparatory Commission (Gibson) to the Secretary of State
div.p: 19. Morning and afternoon sessions entirely inconclusive.

Resolutions were introduced by the BureauThe Bureau of the Preparatory Commission, composed of the president and vice presidents of the Commission, together with officials of the Disarmament Section of the League of Nations Secretariat. to take note of the fact that most delegates declined to accept the principle of Russian
proposals; that some desired to refer them to their governments; that German resolution concerning publicity of armaments be deferred to second reading; and that second reading be left to president to summon at a date not later than next Assembly. No definite action was taken regarding any of them although they were discussed in the utmost confusion.

Russians stated their proposals having been rejected they would cooperate in partial disarmament and to this end the Russian delegation was introducing a new draft for which they demanded immediate first reading.For text of draft, see League of Nations, Documents of the Preparatory Commission, Series VI, p. 347. The president ruled that this draft could not be discussed at this session. Russians objected. President then reserved his ruling declaring it to have been his personal opinion on a point which must be decided by the Commission tomorrow.
There has not been such an entirely futile and unedifying session since the Commission began.
